suggested_outline: The Hidden Cost of 'Reply with JSON': A New LLM Behavior
The One-Word Census: Methodology Revisited
Quantifying the Collapse: Reduced Diversity and Increased Modality
The 'Sharpening' Effect: Distinctive Models are Most Affected
Default Shifts: How JSON Creates New Behavioral Defaults
Practical Implications for Prompt Engineering and Application Design
When to Use Structured Output (and When Not To)
Conclusion: Understanding LLM Nuances Beyond Basic Prompts
topics.summary: This study reveals a critical, often overlooked, aspect of LLM behavior: simply requesting structured output (e.g., JSON) dramatically reduces the diversity of answers, even without schema enforcement or constrained decoding. Re-running the 'One-Word Census' across 44 models, the research shows a sharp convergence towards modal answers, especially impacting models that were previously more distinctive. This has profound implications for engineers relying on LLMs for creative generation, data extraction, or any task where answer variety is desired or assumed.
topics.title: Structured Output Request Significantly Reduces LLM Answer Diversity
topics.why_now: Released within the last 24 hours on arXiv, this paper uncovers a fundamental behavioral shift in LLMs due to a common prompting technique. This insight is immediately actionable for practitioners designing prompts and evaluating outputs, preventing unintended consequences of structured output requests.

suggested_outline: The Challenge of Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA)
Limitations of Frontier LLMs for Graph Navigation
Introducing Search-on-Graph-R1 (SoGR1): A Hybrid Approach
Training Methodology: SFT and RL for Internalized Navigation
Scaffolding with Gold SPARQL Queries: Grounding Trajectories
Performance Benchmarks: SoGR1 vs. Frontier LLMs on Key Datasets
Cost-Benefit Analysis: Efficiency Gains with Compact Models
Practical Applications and Future Directions in KGQA
Conclusion: Democratizing Knowledge Graph Access with Smaller LLMs
topics.summary: This paper introduces Search-on-Graph-R1 (SoGR1), a method for Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA) that internalizes graph navigation into compact 8B language models using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning (RL). By scaffolding a frontier teacher with gold SPARQL queries, SoGR1 generates grounded trajectories against live knowledge graphs. This approach allows smaller, more cost-effective models to surpass larger frontier LLMs in KGQA tasks without auxiliary modules or LLM judges, making advanced knowledge graph querying more accessible.
topics.title: Search-on-Graph-R1: Efficient Knowledge Graph Question Answering with Smaller LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
topics.why_now: Newly published on arXiv, this research provides a significant practical advancement in making KGQA more efficient and cost-effective. It offers a clear path for practitioners to integrate knowledge graphs with smaller, specialized LLMs, moving away from expensive frontier models for navigation.

suggested_outline: The Enterprise Problem: Document Classification Beyond the Lab
Introducing SIFT: Self-Improving, Frozen-gate Training
Hybrid Architecture: Cheap Pipeline + LLM Judge
The Feedback Loop: How the LLM Teaches the Classifier
Ensuring Safety: Critical-Label F1 Regression and Promote Gates
Onboarding New Document Families with Declarative Bundles
Implications for Real-World AI Deployment
Conclusion: Towards Autonomous and Adaptive Classification
topics.summary: This paper introduces SIFT (Self-Improving, Frozen-gate Training), a dynamic document classifier designed to overcome enterprise challenges like expensive labeling and fear of autonomous retraining. SIFT uses a cheap, CPU-bound pipeline for most classifications, escalating low-confidence cases to an LLM judge. The judge's verdicts then continuously teach the cheaper model, reducing escalation rates and improving accuracy over time, with built-in safety mechanisms to prevent silent regressions.
topics.title: SIFT: A Self-Improving Classifier for Dynamic Document Classification in the Enterprise
topics.why_now: Published on arXiv within the last 24 hours, SIFT addresses a critical pain point for applied AI in enterprise settings: how to deploy and continuously improve document classification systems without massive upfront or ongoing human labeling efforts, while maintaining safety.

suggested_outline: Introduction: The Challenge of Robust AI Agent Development
What is Harness Engineering? Overview of the Workflow
Key Components: Planner, Generator, Coding/QA Agents, Goal Review
Ensuring Reliability: Proof of Work and Integration Strategies
Practical Implementation: Setting up and Running a Harness Project
Case Study: Migrating a Monorepo to an Open-Source Stack with Harness
Conclusion: Towards More Reliable and Maintainable Agentic Systems
topics.summary: This project introduces a comprehensive workflow for developing and managing AI agents, emphasizing robust QA, integration, and goal review. It proposes a structured approach to turn high-level goals into verifiable work, using a 'harness' to orchestrate LLM-based coding agents, ensuring that tasks are completed with evidence and persist beyond a single chat session. This is critical for building reliable and maintainable multi-agent systems.
topics.title: Harness Engineering: A Robust Workflow for AI Agent Development
topics.why_now: The project was recently highlighted on Hacker News and provides a concrete, open-source framework for tackling the growing complexity of AI agentic workflows, moving beyond ad-hoc prompting to systematic engineering.

suggested_outline: The Inductive Bias Gap: Locality in Transformers
Why Convolutions? Re-evaluating an Old Technique for LLMs
Methodology: Macro and Micro Ablation Studies in Qwen3
Optimal Placement and Configuration of Depthwise Convolutions
Performance Gains: Accuracy Improvements with Minimal Parameters
Representation-Level Analysis: How Convolutions Affect Context Sensitivity
Implications for LLM Architecture Design and Efficiency
Conclusion: A Hybrid Future for Large Language Models
topics.summary: This research investigates the benefits of integrating lightweight depthwise convolutions into Transformer-based Large Language Models (LLMs). By strategically applying convolutions to projected queries, keys, and values before attention, the study demonstrates improved average accuracy on downstream benchmarks with minimal parameter increase. This suggests that convolutions can provide a valuable local inductive bias, complementing self-attention for modeling short-range token interactions and potentially leading to more efficient or performant LLMs.
topics.title: Convolution for LLMs: Enhancing Locality in Transformer Architectures
topics.why_now: Newly published on arXiv, this paper offers a concrete, low-cost architectural modification to Transformer models, which could be directly applicable to improving existing LLMs. It challenges the pure attention paradigm by re-introducing a classic neural network component.
